title: Indian Independence Act 1947

infobox.value: An Act to make provision for the setting up in India of two independent dominion states, to substitute other provisions for certain provisions of the Government of India Act, 1935, which apply outside those dominions, and to provide for other matters consequential on or connected with the setting up of those Dominions.

[Constitution of India](/wiki/Constitution_of_India "Constitution of India") (India) [Constitution of Pakistan of 1956](/wiki/Constitution_of_Pakistan_of_1956 "Constitution of Pakistan of 1956") (Pakistan)

([10 & 11 Geo. 6](/wiki/10_%26_11_Geo._6 "10 & 11 Geo. 6"). c. 30) is an act of the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") that [partitioned](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India") into the two new independent [dominions](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion") of [India](/wiki/Dominion_of_India "Dominion of India") and [Pakistan](/wiki/Dominion_of_Pakistan "Dominion of Pakistan"). On 15 July 1947 the House of Commons voted for the bill and the House of Lords gave its consent on the next day. The act received Royal Assent on 18 July 1947 and thus modern-day India and Pakistan, comprising [west](/wiki/West_Pakistan "West Pakistan") (modern day [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan")) and [east](/wiki/East_Pakistan "East Pakistan") (modern day [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh")) regions, came into being on 15 August.[[1]](#cite_note-SinghHoshiar-1)[[a]](#cite_note-2)
on what has come to be known as the *3 June Plan* or *Mountbatten Plan.*

Indian Independence Act 1947

The **Indian Independence Act 1947** ([10 & 11 Geo. 6](/wiki/10_%26_11_Geo._6 "10 & 11 Geo. 6"). c. 30) is an act of the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") that [partitioned](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India") into the two new independent [dominions](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion") of [India](/wiki/Dominion_of_India "Dominion of India") and [Pakistan](/wiki/Dominion_of_Pakistan "Dominion of Pakistan"). On 15 July 1947 the House of Commons voted for the bill and the House of Lords gave its consent on the next day. The act received Royal Assent on 18 July 1947 and thus modern-day India and Pakistan, comprising [west](/wiki/West_Pakistan "West Pakistan") (modern day [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan")) and [east](/wiki/East_Pakistan "East Pakistan") (modern day [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh")) regions, came into being on 15 August.[[1]](#cite_note-SinghHoshiar-1)[[a]](#cite_note-2)

The legislature representatives of the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress"),[[2]](#cite_note-3) the [Muslim League](/wiki/All-India_Muslim_League "All-India Muslim League"),[[3]](#cite_note-4) and the [Sikh](/wiki/Sikhs "Sikhs") community[[4]](#cite_note-5) came to an agreement with [Lord Mountbatten](/wiki/Louis_Mountbatten,_1st_Earl_Mountbatten_of_Burma "Louis Mountbatten, 1st Earl Mountbatten of Burma"), then [Viceroy and Governor-General of India](/wiki/Governor-General_of_India "Governor-General of India")**,** on what has come to be known as the *3 June Plan* or *Mountbatten Plan.*

## Prelude

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Independence_Act_1947&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Prelude")]

### Attlee's announcement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Independence_Act_1947&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Attlee's announcement")]

[Clement Attlee](/wiki/Clement_Attlee "Clement Attlee"), the [prime minister of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Prime_minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime minister of the United Kingdom"), announced on 20 February 1947 that:

1. The British Government would grant full self-government to British India by 3 June 1948 at the latest,
2. The future of the [Princely States](/wiki/Princely_States "Princely States") would be decided after the date of final transfer is decided.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

### Third June Plan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Independence_Act_1947&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Third June Plan")]

The 3rd June 1947 Plan was also known as the Mountbatten Plan.
The British government proposed a plan, announced on 3 June 1947, that included these principles:

1. Principle of the partition of British India was accepted by the British Government
2. Successor governments would be given [dominion](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion") status
3. Autonomy and sovereignty to both countries
4. Can make their own constitution
5. Princely states were given the right to join either India or Pakistan (with no option to remain independent), based on two major factors: geographical contiguity and the people's wishes.[[6]](#cite_note-7)

## Provisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Independence_Act_1947&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Provisions")]

The act's most important provisions were:

* Division of British India into the two new dominions – the [Dominion of India](/wiki/Dominion_of_India "Dominion of India") and the [Dominion of Pakistan](/wiki/Dominion_of_Pakistan "Dominion of Pakistan") – with effect from 15 August 1947;
* Partition of the provinces of [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency") and [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab_Province_(British_India) "Punjab Province (British India)") between the two new countries;
* Establishment of the office of [Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General "Governor-General") in each of the two new countries, as representatives of [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown");
* Conferral of complete legislative authority upon the respective [Constituent Assemblies](/wiki/Constituent_Assembly "Constituent Assembly") of the two new countries;
* Termination of British [suzerainty](/wiki/Suzerainty "Suzerainty") over the [princely states](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state"), with effect from 15 August 1947. These states could decide to join either India or Pakistan;[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9)
* Abolition of the use of the title "[Emperor of India](/wiki/Emperor_of_India "Emperor of India")" by the [British monarch](/wiki/British_monarch "British monarch") (this was subsequently executed by [King George VI](/wiki/George_VI_of_the_United_Kingdom "George VI of the United Kingdom") by [royal proclamation](/wiki/Royal_proclamation "Royal proclamation") on 22 June 1948);

The act also made provision for the division of joint property, etc. between the two new countries, including in particular the division of the armed forces.

## Salient features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Independence_Act_1947&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Salient features")]

1. Two new dominion states: Two new dominions were to emerge from the Indian empire: India and Pakistan.
2. Appointed Date: 15 August 1947 was declared as the appointed date for the partition.
3. Territories:
   1. Pakistan: East Bengal, West Punjab, Sindh, and Chief Commissioner's Province of Baluchistan.
   2. The fate of the [North-West Frontier Province](/wiki/North-West_Frontier_Province "North-West Frontier Province") (now [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa")) was subject to the result of a [referendum](/wiki/1947_North-West_Frontier_Province_referendum "1947 North-West Frontier Province referendum").
   3. Bengal & Assam:
      1. The [province of Bengal](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency") as constituted under the Government of India Act 1935 ceased to exist.
      2. In lieu thereof two new provinces were to be constituted, to be known respectively as [East Bengal](/wiki/East_Bengal "East Bengal") and [West Bengal](/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal").
      3. The fate of [District Sylhet](/wiki/Sylhet_Division "Sylhet Division"), in the province of [Assam](/wiki/Assam_Province "Assam Province"), was to be decided in a [referendum](/wiki/1947_Sylhet_referendum "1947 Sylhet referendum").
   4. Punjab:
      1. The [province](/wiki/Punjab_Province_(British_India) "Punjab Province (British India)") as constituted under the Government of India Act 1935 ceased to exist.
      2. Two new provinces were to be constituted, to be known respectively as [West Punjab](/wiki/West_Punjab "West Punjab") and [East Punjab](/wiki/East_Punjab "East Punjab").
4. The boundaries of the new provinces were to be determined, whether before or after the appointed date, by the award of a boundary commission to be appointed by the Governor-General.
5. Constitution for the New Dominions: until the time of the making of the new constitution, the new dominions and the provinces thereof were to be governed by the Government of India Act 1935. (Temporary Provisions as to the Government of Each New Dominion).
6. The Governors-General of the new dominions:
   1. For each of the new dominions a new Governor-General was to be appointed by the Crown, subject to the law of the legislature of either of the new dominions.
   2. Same person as Governor-General of both dominions: if unless and until provision to the contrary was made by a law of the legislature of either of the new dominions, the same person could be the Governor-General of both.
7. Powers of Governor-General: (Section-9)
   1. The Governor-General was empowered to bring this Act into force.
   2. Division of territories, powers, duties, rights, assets, liabilities, etc., was the responsibility of Governor General.
   3. To adopt, amend, Government of India Act 1935, as the Governor-General may consider it necessary.
   4. power to introduce any change was until 31 March 1948, after that it was open to the constituent assembly to modify or adopt the same Act. (Temporary Provisions as to the Government of Each New Dominion.)
   5. Governor-General had full powers to give assent to any law.
8. Legislation for the new dominions:
   1. The existing legislative setup was allowed to continue as Constitution making body as well as a legislature. (Temporary Provisions as to the Government of Each New Dominion.)
   2. The legislature of each dominion was given full powers to make laws for that dominion, including laws having extraterritorial operation.
   3. No Act of Parliament of UK passed after the appointed date would be extended to the territories of new dominions.
   4. No law and provision of any law made by the legislature of the new dominions shall be void or inoperative on the ground that it is repugnant to the law of England.
   5. The Governor-General of each dominion had full powers to give assent in His Majesty's name to any law of the legislature. [Configuration of Pakistan's Constitution Assembly (CAP I): 69 members of the central legislature + 10 immigrant members= 79].
9. Consequences of setting up of the new dominions:
   1. His Majesty's Government lost all the responsibility to the new dominions.
   2. The suzerainty of His Majesty's Government over the Indian States lapsed.
   3. All the treaties or agreements with the Indian States and the [tribal areas](/wiki/Adivasi "Adivasi") that were in force at the passing of the act lapsed.
   4. The title of "Emperor of India" was dropped from the titles of British Crown.
   5. The office of [Secretary of State for India](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_India "Secretary of State for India") was abolished and the provisions of GOI Act 1935 relating to the appointments to the civil service or civil posts under the crown by the secretary of the state ceased to operate.
10. Civil servants: Section 10 provided for the continuance of service of the government servants appointed on or before 15 August 1947 under the Governments of new Dominions with full benefits.
11. Armed Forces: Sections 11, 12, and 13 dealt with the future of the Indian armed forces. A Partition Committee was formed on 7 June 1947, with two representatives from each side and the viceroy in the chair, to decide about the division thereof. As soon as the process of partition was to start it was to be replaced by a Partition Council with a similar structure.
12. First and Second Schedules:
    1. First Schedule listed the districts provisionally included in the new province of East Bengal:
       1. [Chittagong Division](/wiki/Chittagong_Division "Chittagong Division"): Districts of [Chittagong](/wiki/Chittagong_District "Chittagong District"), [Chittagong Hill Tracts](/wiki/Chittagong_Hill_Tracts "Chittagong Hill Tracts"), [Noakhali](/wiki/Noakhali_District "Noakhali District") and [Tipperah](/wiki/Comilla_District "Comilla District").
       2. [Dacca Division](/wiki/Dhaka_Division "Dhaka Division"): Districts of [Bakarganj](/wiki/Backergunge_District "Backergunge District"), [Dacca](/wiki/Dhaka_District "Dhaka District"), [Faridpur](/wiki/Faridpur_Division "Faridpur Division"), and [Mymensingh](/wiki/Mymensingh_Division "Mymensingh Division").
       3. [Presidency Division](/wiki/Presidency_division "Presidency division"): Districts of [Jessore](/wiki/Jessore_District "Jessore District") (except [Bangaon](/wiki/Bangaon_subdivision "Bangaon subdivision") Tehsil), and [Kustia](/wiki/Kushtia_District_(1947%E2%80%931984) "Kushtia District (1947–1984)") and [Meherpur](/wiki/Meherpur_District "Meherpur District") Tehsils (of [Nadia district](/wiki/Nadia_district "Nadia district")).
       4. [Rajshahi Division](/wiki/Rajshahi_Division "Rajshahi Division"):Districts of [Bogra](/wiki/Bogra_District "Bogra District"), [Dinajpur](/wiki/Dinajpur_District,_Bangladesh "Dinajpur District, Bangladesh") (except [Raiganj](/wiki/Uttar_Dinajpur_district "Uttar Dinajpur district") and [Balurghat](/wiki/Dakshin_Dinajpur_district "Dakshin Dinajpur district") Tehsil), [Rajshahi](/wiki/Rajshahi_District "Rajshahi District"), [Rangpur](/wiki/Rangpur_District "Rangpur District") and [Nawabganj](/wiki/Chapai_Nawabganj_District "Chapai Nawabganj District") Tehsil (of [Malda](/wiki/Malda_district "Malda district") district).
    2. Second Schedule listed the districts provisionally included in the new province of West Punjab:

## Partition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Independence_Act_1947&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Partition")]

Main articles: [Partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") and [Radcliffe Line](/wiki/Radcliffe_Line "Radcliffe Line")

There was so much violence, and many Muslims from what would become India fled to Pakistan; and Hindus and Sikhs from what would become Pakistan fled to India. Many people left behind all their possessions and property to avoid the violence and flee to their new country.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

## Princely states

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Independence_Act_1947&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Princely states")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/India_%26_Pakistan_on_eve_of_Independence.png/250px-India_%26_Pakistan_on_eve_of_Independence.png)](/wiki/File:India_%26_Pakistan_on_eve_of_Independence.png)

The territory of British India partitioned between India (brown) and Pakistan (green). The remaining unshaded areas were princely states.

Main articles: [Princely states of Pakistan](/wiki/Princely_states_of_Pakistan "Princely states of Pakistan") and [Princely states of India](/wiki/Princely_states_of_India "Princely states of India")

On 25 July 1947, Mountbatten held a meeting with the [Chamber of Princes](/wiki/Chamber_of_Princes "Chamber of Princes"), where he addressed the question of the princely states, of which there were 562. The treaty relations between Britain and the Indian States would come to an end, and on 15 August 1947 the [suzerainty](/wiki/Suzerainty "Suzerainty") of the British Crown was to lapse. Mountbatten ruled out any dominion status for any of the princely states, and advised them to accede to one or the other of the dominions, India and Pakistan, according to geographical contiguity. Although it was possible for the states to remain independent (autonomous) to some extent after 15 August, their dependency on the British government of dominion of India for defence, foreign affairs, communication and other matters rendered such freedom meaningless.[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Independence_Act_1947&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: India")]

Further information: [Princely states of India](/wiki/Princely_states_of_India "Princely states of India") and [Political integration of India](/wiki/Political_integration_of_India "Political integration of India")

Lord Mountbatten continued as the first Governor General of independent India. [Jawaharlal Nehru](/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru "Jawaharlal Nehru") became the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_India "Prime Minister of India") and [Vallabhbhai Patel](/wiki/Sardar_Vallabhbhai_Patel "Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel") became the [home minister](/wiki/Home_Minister "Home Minister").

Over 550 princely states, almost all of the states contiguous with the territory of India, acceded to India by 15 August. The exceptions were [Junagadh](/wiki/Junagadh_State "Junagadh State"), [Hyderabad](/wiki/Hyderabad_State "Hyderabad State"), and [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(princely_state) "Jammu and Kashmir (princely state)").

Contiguous to both India and Pakistan, the princely state of Jammu and Kashmir, on account of its 77% Muslim majority, was expected to accede to Pakistan. When Maharaja [Hari Singh](/wiki/Hari_Singh "Hari Singh") hesitated to do this, Pakistan launched a [tribal invasion](/wiki/First_Kashmir_War "First Kashmir War") in an effort to persuade him. Instead, he chose to accede to India. The state has been [disputed](/wiki/Kashmir_conflict "Kashmir conflict") between India and Pakistan ever since.[[13]](#cite_note-SteinArnold2010-14)

The state of [Junagadh](/wiki/Junagadh_State "Junagadh State") initially acceded to Pakistan but faced a revolt from its Hindu population. India considered the accession of Junagadh invalid because it violated the principle of geographical contiguity of the partition, but Pakistan argued that the maritime border of Junagadh is connected to Pakistan by sea route. Following a breakdown of law and order, its [Dewan](/wiki/Dewan "Dewan") requested India to take over the administration on 8 November 1947. India conducted a referendum in the state on 20 February 1948, in which the people voted overwhelmingly to join India.

The state of [Hyderabad](/wiki/Hyderabad_state "Hyderabad state") had a majority Hindu population but also a Muslim ruler with a large Muslim minority. The [Nizam of Hyderabad](/wiki/Nizam_of_Hyderabad "Nizam of Hyderabad") wanted to get Dominion status. Hyderabad elected to maintain its independence and lobbied internationally for recognition. However, it faced the pro-communist [Telangana Rebellion](/wiki/Telangana_Rebellion "Telangana Rebellion") and agitation by Indian nationalists opposed to its independence. On 13 September 1948, the Indian government launched an invasion of Hyderabad called [Operation Polo](/wiki/Operation_Polo "Operation Polo"). The Hyderabadi military was defeated over five days of fighting. With his state about to be over run, the Nizam signed the [Instrument of Accession](/wiki/Instrument_of_Accession "Instrument of Accession"), joining India. The formal integration of Hyderabad into the dominion of India took place much later on January 25, 1950, when the Nizam signed the Instrument of Accession with the central government. A day later, as India became a republic on January 26, the Nizam took over as the Raj Pramukh or governor.

### Pakistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Independence_Act_1947&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Pakistan")]

Further information: [Princely states of Pakistan](/wiki/Princely_states_of_Pakistan "Princely states of Pakistan")

[Muhammad Ali Jinnah](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Jinnah "Muhammad Ali Jinnah") became the [governor-general of Pakistan](/wiki/Governor-general_of_Pakistan "Governor-general of Pakistan"), and [Liaquat Ali Khan](/wiki/Liaquat_Ali_Khan "Liaquat Ali Khan") became the [prime minister of Pakistan](/wiki/Prime_minister_of_Pakistan "Prime minister of Pakistan").

Between October 1947 and March 1948 the rulers of several Muslim-majority states signed instruments of accession to [join Pakistan](/wiki/Princely_states_of_Pakistan "Princely states of Pakistan"). These included [Amb](/wiki/Amb_(princely_state) "Amb (princely state)"), [Bahawalpur](/wiki/Bahawalpur_(princely_state) "Bahawalpur (princely state)"), [Chitral](/wiki/Chitral_(princely_state) "Chitral (princely state)"), [Dir](/wiki/Dir_(princely_state) "Dir (princely state)"), [Kalat](/wiki/Khanate_of_Kalat "Khanate of Kalat"), [Khairpur](/wiki/Khairpur_(princely_state) "Khairpur (princely state)"), [Kharan](/wiki/Kharan_(princely_state) "Kharan (princely state)"), [Las Bela](/wiki/Las_Bela_(princely_state) "Las Bela (princely state)"), [Makran](/wiki/Makran_(princely_state) "Makran (princely state)"), and [Swat](/wiki/Swat_(princely_state) "Swat (princely state)").[[14]](#cite_note-Behera-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16) The Khanate of Kalat initially elected to resume its independence, until 27 March 1948 when its ruler acceded to Pakistan.

## Repeal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Independence_Act_1947&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Repeal")]

The Indian Independence Act was subsequently repealed in Article 395 of the [Constitution of India](/wiki/Constitution_of_India "Constitution of India") and in Article 221 of the [Constitution of Pakistan of 1956](/wiki/Constitution_of_Pakistan_of_1956 "Constitution of Pakistan of 1956"),[[16]](#cite_note-17) both constitutions being intended to bring about greater independence for the new states. The act has not been repealed in the United Kingdom, where it still has an effect, although some sections of it have been repealed.
